transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Mia, I’m glad you’re here today. What’s on your mind that feels most important to share?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately. It’s just that I received an email from my ex-partner about child support, and it’s really weighing on me. I can’t help but feel anxious about the whole situation. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of worry, and I don’t know how to break free from it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that email from your ex-partner has really triggered a lot of anxiety for you, and it’s understandable to feel overwhelmed by it. You mentioned feeling stuck in a cycle of worry—can you tell me more about what that feels like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... every time I think about it, I start to spiral. I keep telling myself that he’s not willing to help, and then I worry about money and how I’m going to manage everything on my own. It feels like I’m going to run out of options, and that thought just makes me feel even more anxious. I try to push myself to be independent, but sometimes it just feels like too much. I don’t know if I’m really getting anywhere.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the thoughts about your ex-partner's lack of support and the financial worries are really weighing heavily on you. I hear that you’re trying to be independent, but it feels overwhelming at times, and you’re questioning if you’re making progress. When you find yourself in that spiral, what kind of thoughts or feelings come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, it’s like a mix of fear and sadness. I think about how I might not have enough money, and then I feel scared about what that means for my future. I also feel really lonely sometimes, like I’m carrying this burden all by myself. I guess I just wish things were different, and it’s hard to see a way out when I’m in that headspace. I try to reach out to family for support, but even then, it feels like I’m just trying to hold it all together.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to carry that mix of fear and sadness, especially when you feel so alone in it. The burden of financial worries and the longing for things to be different can be really heavy. It’s good that you’re reaching out to family for support, but I can imagine it still feels like a lot to manage on your own. When you think about holding it all together, what do you feel you need most right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think... I just need some reassurance that I’m not going to end up in a really bad place. It would help to know that I can figure this out, even if it takes time. Sometimes I feel like I’m just trying to keep my head above water, and it would be nice to have someone to lean on a bit more. I guess I want to feel more secure, both financially and emotionally. It’s hard to see that right now, though.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you’re seeking reassurance and a sense of security, both financially and emotionally. Feeling like you’re just trying to keep your head above water can be exhausting, and wanting someone to lean on is completely natural. It sounds like having that support and a clearer path forward would really help you feel more grounded. When you think about the support you need, what does that look like for you? What would make you feel more secure?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think having someone who understands what I’m going through would be really helpful. Just being able to talk about my worries without feeling judged or like I’m burdening them. Maybe some practical advice on how to manage my finances better would help too. I guess I just want to feel like I have a plan, something to work towards, rather than just feeling lost. It would be nice to know that I’m not alone in this, that there are people who can help me navigate through it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like having a supportive person who truly understands your situation would make a big difference for you. Being able to talk openly about your worries without judgment is so important, and it’s great that you’re thinking about practical steps to manage your finances. Wanting a clear plan to work towards can help reduce that feeling of being lost. 

As you think about this support, are there specific people in your life you feel comfortable reaching out to, or perhaps resources you might consider exploring?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I have a couple of family members I could reach out to, like my sister. She’s always been supportive, and I think she’d be willing to listen. I just sometimes hesitate because I don’t want to burden her with my problems. As for resources, I’ve thought about looking into some financial counseling or support groups, but I haven’t really taken that step yet. It feels a bit daunting, you know? I guess I’m just trying to figure out the best way to approach it without feeling overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s great to hear that you have a supportive sister you can reach out to. It’s completely understandable to hesitate about sharing your struggles, as you don’t want to feel like a burden. It sounds like you’re also considering some practical steps, like financial counseling or support groups, but the thought of taking that step feels daunting. 

What do you think might help you feel more comfortable reaching out to your sister or exploring those resources? Is there a smaller step you could take that feels more manageable?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by just sending a text to my sister, just to check in and see how she’s doing. That feels less intimidating than having a full conversation right away. If she seems open, I could gradually share what I’m going through. As for the resources, maybe I could just do a little research online first, like looking up some local financial counseling options, before committing to anything. Taking those smaller steps might make it feel less overwhelming, I think.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That sounds like a really thoughtful approach. Starting with a simple text to your sister can open the door for support without feeling too overwhelming, and it’s great that you’re considering gradually sharing your feelings. Doing some online research for financial counseling is also a manageable first step that can help you feel more informed and prepared. 

How do you feel about taking those smaller steps? Does it give you a sense of relief or hope to think about moving forward in that way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it does give me a bit of relief to think about taking those smaller steps. It feels more manageable, like I’m not trying to tackle everything all at once. Just reaching out to my sister and doing some research feels like a way to regain a bit of control over the situation. I guess it gives me a little hope that I can start to find some support and maybe even a clearer path forward. It’s nice to think that I don’t have to face this all alone.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m really glad to hear that! Taking those smaller steps can definitely help you feel more in control and less overwhelmed. It’s encouraging to know that reaching out for support and doing some research can bring you a sense of hope and connection. 

As you move forward with these steps, how do you think you might remind yourself to stay focused on this progress, especially when those feelings of anxiety or overwhelm creep back in?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could try to remind myself that it’s okay to take things one step at a time. Maybe I could write down my thoughts or feelings when I start to feel overwhelmed, just to get them out of my head. It might help to have a little reminder, like a note or something, to keep me focused on the steps I want to take. I could also try to practice some self-compassion, reminding myself that it’s normal to feel anxious and that I’m doing my best. Just having those little reminders might help me stay grounded when things get tough.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Those sound like wonderful strategies! Taking things one step at a time and writing down your thoughts can be really helpful in managing overwhelm. Having reminders of self-compassion and acknowledging that it’s okay to feel anxious can create a more supportive inner dialogue for yourself. 

It’s great that you’re thinking about ways to stay grounded during tough moments. As you prepare to take these steps, is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss today that might help you feel even more supported?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind today. It feels good to talk about these steps and have a plan, even if it’s just a starting point. I guess I just want to make sure I keep this momentum going and don’t fall back into that cycle of worry. Maybe just having a follow-up plan for our next session could help me stay accountable. I appreciate having this space to share and think things through.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m really glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful and that you have a plan to start with. It’s completely understandable to want to maintain that momentum and stay accountable. 

For our next session, we could check in on how reaching out to your sister goes and any insights you gain from your research on financial counseling. We can also explore any challenges that come up as you take these steps. How does that sound to you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: That sounds good to me. I think checking in on those things will help me stay focused and accountable. I appreciate having a space to talk about what I’m going through and to explore these steps together. It feels a bit easier knowing I have a plan and someone to share it with. Thank you for your support today.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Mia. I’m glad you feel supported and that we could explore these steps together. It’s important to have a plan and someone to share it with, and I’m here for you as you move forward. I look forward to hearing how things go in our next session. Take care of yourself in the meantime, and remember that it’s okay to take things one step at a time.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. I’ll do my best to take things one step at a time and keep you updated on how things go. It feels good to have this support, and I’ll try to take care of myself in the meantime. Looking forward to our next session. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Mia. I’m glad you’re feeling supported, and I’m looking forward to hearing about your progress in our next session. Take care of yourself, and remember to be gentle with yourself as you navigate these steps. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I will definitely keep that in mind. I appreciate your understanding and support. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Mia! I’m glad to be here for you. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you soon! Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Take care, Mia! See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Take care! Looking forward to it!
